title: Poulet Rôti
subtitle: Simple French roast chicken
excerpt: A whole chicken roasted at high heat with butter, lemon, thyme, and garlic. No basting required. The skin blisters and crisps; the meat stays juicy. The simplest possible Sunday chicken.
sourceNotes: Poulet rôti simple is documented in Jacques Pépin, La Technique (1976) and Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960). The French approach (high heat throughout, no liquid in the pan, a brief rest) differs from slower British roasting methods.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Season the chicken and leave uncovered in the fridge overnight for even crispier skin — the salt dries the surface.

content.text: The best roast chicken is the simplest one. Butter under and over the skin, a lemon squeezed into the cavity, thyme and garlic tucked inside, and an oven at 220°C fan for 70 minutes. The skin blisters and turns deep gold; the fat runs out into the pan; the juices that collect are the only sauce you need.

Do not baste. Opening the oven drops the temperature and steams the skin. High, consistent heat is the method. Rest the bird for 15 minutes after roasting — this is not optional; the juices need time to redistribute.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Pat the chicken dry inside and out. Gently loosen the skin over the breast with your fingers and push half the butter under the skin, spreading it across the breast meat. Rub the remaining butter all over the outside. Season generously with salt and pepper, inside the cavity and outside. Squeeze the lemon halves into the cavity and put the squeezed halves inside with the garlic and thyme.

Place breast-side up in a roasting pan and roast at 220°C for 65 to 70 minutes. The skin should be deep gold and crisp; the juices should run clear when the thigh is pierced at the thickest point. Do not open the oven before 60 minutes. Rest on a board loosely tented with foil for 15 minutes before carving. Serve with the pan juices poured over.

items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or bird was wet before going in
items.fix: Pat very dry before buttering; ensure the oven is fully at temperature before the chicken goes in

items.cause: Overcooked, or not enough butter under the skin
items.fix: Use a thermometer: 73°C internal in the thigh is done; rest for the full 15 minutes — dry breast is often a resting problem

content.text: The French rôtisserie (a rotating spit in front of a heat source) is the original form, and rotisserie chickens from French butchers and markets are still among the best fast food available anywhere. The home oven version approximates the result through high heat and good chicken. In France, the chicken's quality matters enough that Bresse chickens (poulet de Bresse, France's only AOC bird) command a significant premium.
